Regulators say crypto ETFs are designed to solve one of the biggest challenges facing retail and institutional investors: security and complexity. By offering exposure through regulated funds, investors can avoid managing private keys or digital wallets—long viewed as major deterrents to broader adoption.

“A key advantage of crypto ETFs is ease of access,” Kongsakul noted, emphasizing that the structure significantly reduces hacking and operational risks while offering a familiar investment vehicle for traditional market participants.

To support smooth trading, the SEC is also considering introducing market makers for crypto ETFs, potentially drawing participation from financial institutions, digital asset exchanges, corporations, and crypto-holding entities to ensure liquidity.

🇹🇭 Thailand plans to expand its crypto ETF market beyond Bitcoin to include multiple tokens, with new rules expected early next year.#Thailand#CryptoETFshttps://t.co/ob28LM5N0g

— Cryptonews.com (@cryptonews) October 2, 2025

Crypto futures and portfolio strategy

Beyond ETFs, Thailand’s regulator plans to formally recognize digital assets as an underlying asset class under the Derivatives Act, enabling crypto futures trading on the Thailand Futures Exchange (TFEX). Officials say futures contracts would give investors access to hedging and risk management tools, positioning crypto alongside more mature financial instruments.

The SEC has been careful to frame digital assets not as speculative bets, but as part of a diversified portfolio. Regulators recommend that investors with higher risk tolerance allocate 4–5% of their portfolios to crypto, reinforcing the narrative that digital assets are becoming a permanent fixture in modern investment strategies.

Thailand approved its first spot Bitcoin ETF in 2024 through One Asset Management, structured as a fund-of-funds aimed at institutional investors. The upcoming expansion into altcoin ETFs marks the next phase, following Bloomberg’s October 2025 report that Thai regulators were drafting rules to broaden exposure beyond Bitcoin.

Tokenization, influencers, and global momentum

The SEC is also collaborating with the Bank of Thailand on a regulatory sandbox for tokenization and distributed ledger technology, with plans to expand digital tokens beyond investment products to include bond tokens, tokenized fund units, and ESG-linked green tokens.

🇹🇭 Thailand chooses KuCoin as lead partner for historic $153M tokenized government securities with $3 minimum investment.#Thailand#Cryptohttps://t.co/do6x6GRhEE

— Cryptonews.com (@cryptonews) August 27, 2025

At the same time, regulators are tightening oversight of online financial personalities, drawing clearer lines between general market commentary and licensed investment advice. Any recommendation tied to securities or returns, Kongsakul warned, will require proper authorization.

Thailand’s regulatory push mirrors a wider global shift toward legitimizing crypto through traditional finance. South Korea plans to introduce spot Bitcoin ETFs in 2026, Vietnam has launched a high-bar crypto licensing pilot, and Vanguard recently opened its $11 trillion brokerage platform to third-party crypto ETFs—underscoring how digital assets are moving from the fringe to the financial mainstream.

data.title: 2026 Crypto Crime Report
data.description:
2025 will be a pivotal year for the cryptocurrency industry, marked by both regulatory transformation and structural progress.

The Trump administration, through a series of pro-crypto policies (such as the "Strengthening American Leadership in Digital Financial Technology" executive order signed in January 2025, the establishment of a strategic Bitcoin reserve in March, the GENIUS Act stablecoin bill signed in July, and the ongoing CLARITY Act market structure bill), is fulfilling its political promises, providing a clearer regulatory path for the crypto industry, and propelling the United States to become the global "crypto capital."

Meanwhile, the Hong Kong government is actively advancing its virtual asset regulatory framework, officially implementing the Stablecoin Ordinance in August 2025, requiring stablecoin issuers to obtain licenses and strengthening the regulation of VASP platforms, further consolidating its position as an Asian Web3 hub.

These favorable global policies spurred accelerated adoption by individual investors, institutional investors, and sovereign nations, pushing Bitcoin to a high of $126,000 and maintaining the industry's total market capitalization at a high of approximately $3 trillion, marking the transformation of crypto assets from the fringe to mainstream finance. However, along with the booming industry and mature infrastructure, while decentralized exchanges (DEXs), cross-chain bridges, DeFi protocols, and various cryptocurrency tools are widely used, cryptocurrencies, with their high liquidity, rapid transfer, and relative anonymity, have also become a target for criminals. With the widespread application of crypto assets and on-chain protocols, the risk of their abuse in some illegal and high-risk activities is gradually emerging. Related infrastructure mainly serves functions such as value transfer, fund mixing, and storage of illicit assets in scenarios such as online gambling, money laundering, black market transactions, fraud, and sanctions evasion. This trend is receiving continuous attention from regulatory agencies. According to Bitrace's monitoring and statistics, in 2025, the total amount of illicit funds received by blockchain addresses marked as high-risk reached $502.1 billion. This report aims to remind the industry that while embracing innovation, it must strengthen compliance and risk control by disclosing the trends and scale of cryptocurrency crime in 2025. Trends

Cryptocurrency Sanctions Amid Geopolitical Conflict

In 2025, the conflict between Israel and Palestine entered its third year. Against this backdrop, Israel continued to crack down on cryptocurrency channels suspected of providing funding to Hamas, Hezbollah, and Iranian-related entities.

According to Bitrace's monitoring, Israel's National Counter-Terrorism Financing Authority (NBCTF) sanctioned at least 446 blockchain addresses or exchange accounts in 2025 alone, mainly involving USDT and Bitcoin, far exceeding other countries.

Online gambling platforms, by building their own cryptocurrency settlement channels or connecting to third-party cryptocurrency payment tools, have achieved a business address structure similar to centralized cryptocurrency exchanges. Gamblers do not directly purchase and convert trading chips into cash using fiat currency, but instead use cryptocurrencies (especially stablecoins) as intermediaries, thus achieving a high degree of anonymity.

Over the past few years, this system has primarily operated stably on the Tron and Ethereum networks. Although lower than in previous years, high-risk gambling addresses still received $145.3 billion in 2025, down from $217.8 billion in 2024.

In the past few years, this system has primarily operated stably on the Tron and Ethereum networks.

The adoption of these stablecoins almost entirely occurred on the Tron network. In 2025, addresses flagged as high-risk black market transactions received a total of $186.9 billion, of which $186.7 billion came from the Tron network.

Despite a slight decrease in the overall size of the black market compared to the previous year, cryptocurrency-based escrow platforms continue to grow despite strong crackdowns by law enforcement agencies in various countries. According to Bitrace, in 2025, escrow addresses on various escrow platforms collected a total of 10.9 billion USDT. Due to the different escrow rules of various platforms, this figure does not fully reflect the true scale of escrow transactions. Actual transactions occur at the business addresses of the escrow merchants, and the scale should far exceed the deposits. Due to sanctions imposed by the UK and the US on Cambodia's Prince Group and Huionepay Group, Huionepay Group was forced to temporarily suspend operations of its main business, Huionepay. According to Bitrace's statistics, Huionepay collected 52.4 billion USDT that year, but by December, its on-chain activity had almost completely ceased. This incident has disrupted the local black market, preventing some operators from withdrawing funds from Huiwang Payment. The industry has lost a stable and secure payment tool. Combined with ongoing law enforcement activities from various countries and the crackdown on Telegram, organized crime networks in East and Southeast Asia are currently in a state of chaos and disorder. Cryptocurrency fraud includes both direct financial deception targeting native crypto users and investment scams targeting non-crypto investors. Both types of illegal fraud are key targets of law enforcement agencies in various countries. In 2025, Bitrace tracked $30 billion in funds linked to addresses involved in real fraud cases, stablecoin freezes, and address poisoning activities, a significant decrease compared to 2024.

With the increase in law enforcement cooperation between Tether and Circle, the scale of stablecoin activity related to frozen addresses has reached an all-time high. In 2025, these addresses received a total of $18.3 billion worth of stablecoins.

As a result of the increased law enforcement cooperation between Tether and Circle, the scale of stablecoin activity related to frozen addresses has reached an all-time high. In 2025, these addresses received a total of $18.3 billion worth of stablecoins.

Bitrace primarily compiled statistics on national-level sanctions imposed by five countries: the United States, Japan, the United Kingdom, Israel, and France. Between 2021 and 2025, five countries (excluding centralized cryptocurrency exchange accounts) imposed sanctions on at least 1,683 blockchain addresses. The US-based OFAC had the most, followed by Israel's NBCTF. Most of Israel's sanctions occurred during the conflict and primarily targeted cryptocurrency addresses suspected of funding Hamas, Hezbollah, and Iranian-related entities. Further analysis of the sanctions activities of Israel's NBCTF between 2021 and 2025 reveals that Tron has a total of 585 network addresses, far exceeding other networks, indicating that terrorist financing is currently making extensive use of Tron network assets. Freezing access to USDT or USDC for specific blockchain addresses is the primary means of law enforcement cooperation between Tether and Circle, the two major stablecoin issuers. Even after excluding some contract addresses and abnormal addresses, the number of addresses frozen in 2025 still reached a record 4,059, exceeding the total of 3,199 frozen addresses in the previous four years. Among them, 653 Ethereum addresses were frozen and 3,406 Tron addresses were frozen, with the latter almost entirely executed by Tether. Further statistics on the amount of money frozen in addresses show that in 2025, Tether successfully froze over 1.1 billion USDT, and Circle successfully froze over 23 million USDC. Circle's law enforcement cooperation activities were significantly lower than Tether's.

data.title: Interview with Jeff Ma: The Three-Year Journey to A9 by a 2004 Prodigy Trader
data.description:
The cryptocurrency world seems to never lack young, talented traders, like Jeff Ma, our interviewee today. This college student achieved a remarkable leap in assets from A6 to A9 in just three years. Even after experiencing margin calls and losses, he still managed to reach a peak personal fortune of 1 billion RMB. His growth trajectory is legendary, achieving exponential wealth growth through a high-leverage rolling strategy. Having participated in numerous Bitget trading competitions and achieved impressive results, behind the impressive numbers lie countless sleepless nights, the high-pressure environment of 24/7 monitoring, and the unwavering willpower to recover from repeated margin calls. Through this interview, BlockBeats attempts to reconstruct Jeff Ma's trading system. He is both an aggressive trader who dares to go all-in with 40x leverage and a fund manager who deeply understands the importance of risk control; he acknowledges the role of luck in wealth accumulation while also emphasizing the value of discipline and systematic thinking. This isn't a "get rich overnight" story, but rather the arduous journey of a young trader's growth and evolution in a high-risk market. The following is the full interview:

From CS:GO Arbitrage to Crypto Trading

Q: You're a young trader born in 2004. What sparked your passion for trading, especially as a college student?

Jeff Ma: All my trading systems are self-taught. I genuinely love trading; only true passion can drive you to go the furthest and achieve the greatest results. If you don't love trading, you can't do it well; you have to love this industry.

I started with CS:GO item trading. But that market was quite limited and lacked depth.

Moreover, it's subject to the game company's control. If Valve, the developer, wants to curb this market and prevent scalpers or resellers from profiting, it can simply issue a negative policy, causing a massive loss of market value overnight. So initially, I was very cautious. I didn't dare to immediately start reselling; instead, I researched cross-region trading opportunities. I discovered that CS:GO has different price zones, with Argentina being a low-price zone at the time. Cross-region trading isn't as easy as it used to be, but back then, whenever the official release of new items, I would use my USD card to buy a cross-region account, purchasing official game items at a lower cost. After the 7+7 day trading cooldown period, I would sell them immediately—simple arbitrage, similar to arbitrage in cryptocurrency exchanges. Initially, I bought these items for personal use, just to play with, but they could be resold. Then, CS:GO received a major update (now renamed CS2), and I instantly made a profit of almost 30%. So at that moment, I realized: this game could make money, and real money at that, more practical than what my teachers taught me in school. I started researching how to earn more, which became the fastest way for me to improve my life. Many of my classmates probably wanted to take the postgraduate entrance exam or the civil service exam to get stable jobs, but I already sensed that the overall economic climate wasn't good. I realized I had to do something different, not be like most people. I felt that what I learned in school wasn't very valuable. I had to become a rare talent, doing things that ordinary people couldn't imitate or that others couldn't replace. And trading is something with that kind of scarcity. Later, a friend introduced me to the cryptocurrency world, and I slowly started trading in cryptocurrencies. Q: You mentioned that you first started by copy trading the trader "I've always dreamed of making a million since I was little." For a trading novice, identifying truly capable people among thousands of traders is extremely difficult. How did you discover this person? What metrics made you think he was worth following—return rate, win rate, maximum drawdown, or something else? Jeff Ma: Yes, a friend introduced me to the trades, but I was just copy trading off-exchange, following the legendary trader I mentioned before, "A Millionaire's Dream Since Childhood." My friend entered the market early, watching "A Millionaire's Dream Since Childhood" grow his capital step by step. Crucially, even at his lowest point, when his trading performance was at its worst, he only lost half his capital; he never experienced a margin call. This is extremely important. At that time, he already had a large trading volume, and many of his followers criticized him for the drawdowns, so he shut down all his public channels. But precisely after shutting them down, he took off. He traded right-side breakouts, and his return rate and drawdown data were excellent. At the time, I didn't understand what rolling over positions was, but his swing trading was excellent, using a right-side breakout strategy with narrow stop-losses. Except for a very few cases, he almost always used small stop-losses. I continue this approach now, except I adjust my profit targets based on different market conditions. He left the network about a year ago. Before leaving, there was a classic market move—BTC was at 72,000, 73,000, and 74,000. He shorted at 72,000, and it later dropped to over 50,000. Although he exited early, that was his last trade before leaving the network, and I still remember it. After that, he completely disappeared from the network, no longer offering trading signals in either public or private domains. I haven't seen him return, and it's basically impossible to find him now. He only traded BTC and ETH, which is what I admire most about him. Because this is the only way to truly test skill – altcoins might have insider information, or market manipulators might deliberately lure retail investors into copying their trades, turning followers into liquidity traps. But this won't happen with Bitcoin; trading BTC and ETH is a true test of skill.

Q: Copy trading and independent trading are two completely different levels. How did you transition from simply copying trades to becoming an independent trader who can understand and replicate trading logic? Including after your initial liquidation, you started learning options trading. Options trading is a very difficult area even for traders with financial backgrounds. As a young person without a financial background, how did you learn this knowledge on your own? What specific channels did you use to learn? What books or courses did you read? How long did it take you from knowing nothing to being able to apply options strategies in practice?

Jeff Ma: Simply put, I learned from books, from successful traders, and from the market.

I read various books and watched videos, and chose a suitable trading system based on my own style.

At the time, I asked others for book recommendations, but purely from a technical perspective, "Japanese Candlestick Charting" was enough for me, perhaps equivalent to what many call "naked candlestick trading." I think this is the best technical indicator—naked candlesticks based on chart patterns. I never learn from those who haven't achieved results. Many teachers haven't achieved significant results themselves; they only talk about other people's cases—like Livermore or the story of some legendary trader. But they haven't practiced themselves, or achieved huge results. I'm unwilling to learn from such people. I'd rather trade myself, even if I lose, at least I'll be a practitioner. I learned quickly because while learning, I also started practicing directly with small amounts of capital. When I did get wiped out, I was very anxious. I didn't want to slowly recover my losses; I wanted to find a very good strategy and rebuild a more powerful trading system. I learned that options have very high returns, especially expiration options, but the risks are also very high. So my learning ability exploded, and I mastered it and applied it in practice in a very short time. I can only say that I might have had some talent, and I was also lucky at the time, successfully implementing some newly learned strategies in the trading environment at the time. Generally speaking, I didn't expect to make money so quickly; there was definitely an element of luck involved. Otherwise, when I first learned options, my capital was relatively small, so I went all-in, mainly buying end-of-term options with only 1 to 3 days of settlement time, and they were out-of-the-money options, not in-the-money options. However, I didn't want my orders to be monitored by others. Even large funds trading spot or futures might use "iceberg orders"—breaking large orders into many smaller orders and buying them in batches to prevent others from easily seeing the movement through the data. This is a way to protect large funds. Q: You mentioned using 40x leverage for rolling over positions on ETH, reaching A7 in February 2024 through BTC rolling over, and breaking through A8 in November 2024 through ETH rolling over. While the rolling over strategy is highly profitable, it carries extremely high risk. Could you explain your rolling over logic in detail? What are your specific entry point selection criteria? How do you set stop-loss and take-profit orders? Under what market conditions would you choose to initiate rolling over? How do you control the position size for each trade in terms of money management? Jeff Ma: The core of my rolling over strategy is to confidently and heavily buy the dip on the left side, not in batches, but by heavily buying the dip in futures contracts. Even if I've already bought a batch in the spot market, if the market is still falling, I will convert the spot to coin-based or USDT-based contracts and start directly buying the dip heavily. Generally, the actual leverage is 5 to 10 times. Then, rolling over positions involves adding to positions with unrealized profits. For example, if the market bottoms out and rebounds, and I buy at the very bottom, and it continues to rise, I will make judgments based on various technical indicators, fundamentals, and macroeconomics before adding to my position. Adding to the position is usually half of the initial position. I used to be more aggressive, using half or even the same position as the initial position, whether it was with 40x leverage or when I rolled over my Bitcoin position in February 2024, I used the most aggressive rolling strategy. Now it might be called adding to positions with unrealized profits, not rolling over, because I have toned it down considerably, and the additions are smaller. Regarding stop-loss and take-profit orders, I usually set a break-even stop-loss when Bitcoin has a 1 to 2 point unrealized profit, placing the stop-loss slightly above my cost price. Bitcoin is currently around $100, so I'm only making enough to cover transaction fees. If this trade is wrong, I won't lose my principal; it's like waking up from a dream after adding to a winning position. Even with the recent market conditions, I've been rolling over positions, and it's failed. The key is that my large profits come from one-sided trends, not from swing trading, scalping, or day trading; they all come from a single large market move. Rolling over positions is the strategy that generates the most profit, but it's also the strategy that causes the most losses. Consecutive stop-losses are extremely painful. Besides rolling over positions, another frequently used strategy is swing trading, which might not be intraday but rather holding positions for several days, with a profit/loss ratio of approximately 1.5 to 3. Both use narrow stop-losses—typically 1 point for Bitcoin and 1.5 to 2 points for Ethereum. In terms of capital allocation, I currently allocate 60% to spot trading, 20% to futures, and 20% to options. Why I Chose Bitget Q: You've participated in several Bitget trading competitions, such as fourth place in the 2025 King of Kings Global Invitational Tournament (KCGI) and 60th place in the 2024 KCGI. How have these competition experiences helped improve your trading skills? Have Bitget's trading competition mechanism, leaderboard system, and real-time verification function helped you build trading credibility? Jeff Ma: In participating in many Bitget trading competitions, my biggest takeaway wasn't how strong I was, but rather a growing awareness that this market is never short of geniuses. You'll see many highly skilled traders on the leaderboard, but the question is, can geniuses stay in this market long-term? Many people only win for a period of time but fail to go further. I always remind myself of Liang Xi's example. Before his account wiped out, he was undoubtedly a highly talented trader in the market, but since then, he hasn't been able to return to his former form for a long time. For me, this precisely illustrates one point: trading isn't just about making a profit once, but more importantly, about holding onto that profit. Money can never be fully earned, but losses can happen very quickly. If you can't lock in profits in time, even the most impressive profit curve can be wiped out in a single misstep. In these competitions, I've seen too many peers and so-called genius traders who are often just flashes in the pan, finding it difficult to survive long-term in this market. This has made me more aware that there are always people better than you, and there's always something beyond your reach. I don't consider myself one of the best traders, but at least I've learned to be aggressive when necessary and to choose a relatively conservative strategy when uncertainty is high, rather than blindly pursuing maximum returns. As for the trading competitions themselves, they do indeed help improve personal visibility and trading reputation to some extent. Many exchanges regularly host contract-related events, but Bitget's competitions are relatively richer in terms of mechanisms and formats. They go beyond simple rankings, incorporating leaderboards and real-time verification systems to ensure your performance is visible and verifiable. Furthermore, its incentive design is diverse, offering not only cash rewards but also a points system that can be redeemed for virtual currency, mobile phones, luxury goods, and even travel rewards. Recent events, for example, included physical prizes like LV bags. This design transforms trading from a cold, numerical competition into a more immersive experience, making it easier to retain top traders within the ecosystem.

Question: There are many cryptocurrency exchanges, including Binance, OKX, and Bybit, which are mainstream choices. What ultimately led you to choose Bitget as your primary trading platform? For example, from a high-frequency trader's perspective, what unique advantages does Bitget offer in terms of product design, trading depth, slippage control, and funding rates? Also, you mentioned in a previous video that you no longer recommend using Little Fox Wallet but instead use Bitget Wallet. What do you find advantageous about Bitget Wallet?

Jeff Ma: I don't really like to describe different exchanges as "better" in terms of which is better. More accurately, it's about which one is more suitable for my trading habits and capital size at the current stage. Binance, OKX, and Bybit are all very mature mainstream platforms, each with its own advantages. In terms of functional coverage, Bitget has now integrated with a system similar to Tradestation, launching the TradFi section, which allows trading US stock indices, gold, silver, and various commodities, making it quite similar to Bybit. However, what truly keeps me on Bitget long-term isn't a single function, but rather the overall stability of the experience and the low cost of use. On one hand, the platform's activity frequency is indeed high, with consistent trading competitions, incentive programs, and various rewards. On the other hand, the platform's speed and responsiveness in service make me feel more comfortable, which is crucial in high-frequency trading. When your trading pace is fast, any minor inconvenience will be magnified. From a trading perspective, in terms of market depth, slippage control, and overall fee structure, my personal experience is that it's closer to a mature centralized exchange system. In contrast, while many on-chain perp DEXs offer advantages in transparency—all data, rankings, and trading activity are publicly available—essentially, people can see the results, but they don't know who you are. In actual use, I've also clearly felt that, in terms of fee structure and overall execution efficiency, on-chain products are still difficult to completely replace centralized exchanges, especially with larger transaction volumes. Regarding wallets, as I mentioned in a previous video, I don't currently recommend frequent use of general-purpose hot wallets like Little Fox. For me, large sums are still kept in cold wallets, such as imToken; for daily operations, I only use a few relatively stable wallets with clearly defined functions, such as Bitget Wallet. One aspect that I find particularly useful about Bitget Wallet is its natural integration with actual consumption scenarios. Its built-in U-card is very convenient for small-amount transactions, such as daily expenses under $200, which can be swiped directly without frequent withdrawals. With regular international cards, whether Visa or Mastercard, there's usually a fee of around 3%, but with the U-card, you essentially save that cost – spend it and it's done, and the process is simpler. Additionally, Bitget Wallet has an Earn Center that periodically launches airdrops or lightly participatory activities. I occasionally participate myself and recommend it to my followers. Although sometimes I get scammed, haha, overall the barrier to entry is low, the operation is smooth, and it's something you can do incidentally. For me, these features aren't necessarily core, but all these features combined make the overall user experience more complete. Q: What conveniences has Bitget brought you? What specific support do they offer in supporting outstanding traders? Jeff Ma: I've actually participated in several offline events organized by Bitget, and they're generally quite well-organized and substantial. For example, I attended the Shanghai event in their city-wide series. This series started in Changsha, then moved to Chengdu, and now Shanghai. For me, the value of these events isn't in the format, but in the exchange itself. Sitting down with these peers to talk about trading and the market is comfortable and significantly broadens one's cognitive horizons. In these offline exchanges, you'll find that participants aren't just from the crypto world. Some peers primarily work in US stocks or institutional fund management, and crypto assets aren't actually the largest component of their overall portfolio. Communicating with these people allows you to learn a lot about the logic and perspectives of different markets, such as how they view macroeconomic cycles, how they select targets, and how they make trade-offs between different assets. This content may not give you direct answers, but it provides very valuable references for your subsequent judgments. Bitget's support system for outstanding traders and high-net-worth users is quite clear. For higher VIP levels, there are dedicated events and benefits, such as free tickets to the Singapore F1 race and Token2049 in September and October; and recent plans for similar island trips. Even VIP3 users regularly enjoy airdrops and exclusive benefits. This support is not one-off but continuous, and the experience is particularly noticeable for users who are consistently active and trade stably on the platform. How can trading novices establish trading discipline? Q: You founded Ma Shen Capital, and the transition from individual trader to capital manager is a significant one. What is the current management and team size of Ma Shen Capital? What are the future development plans? What role do you envision Ma Shen Capital playing in the cryptocurrency investment field? Jeff Ma: I'm very restrained in my choice of partners. I only collaborate with people I know in person, especially when it involves large sums of money. It's impossible for me to discuss financial cooperation online or via private messages; that's a bottom line. I can't disclose the size of my funds; I currently have one partner, but I remain the primary trader and fund manager. In terms of positioning, I never make horizontal comparisons with any institution or individual. My only competitor is myself. Whether it's the crypto market, US stocks, gold, or commodities, what I care about is whether this year's data is better than last year's. Even if the returns aren't higher, as long as the drawdown is smaller and the risk control is more stable, that's progress in my eyes. When the amount of capital increases, the trading style will inevitably change. Previously, when I traded on my own, I could go all in, use heavy leverage, and be very aggressive, but this is unacceptable when managing funds. The importance of risk control will be magnified infinitely. As long as you don't lose money, you've already outperformed most people in any market; if you can consistently achieve an annualized return of 20% over the long term, that's already excellent fund performance. Of course, I still care about returns, but now it's more important to control the maximum drawdown to within 20% through hedging and structural adjustments.

Q: As a young trader who reached A9 level at 21, what advice do you have for beginners? How can they avoid the pitfalls you've taken? What core qualities do you think a good cryptocurrency trader needs? What percentage of your current trading ability comes from books/courses, and how much comes from market "tuition"? Which stage is the most difficult to break through from A6 to A9?

Jeff Ma: The first thing is always to avoid liquidation. The most direct method is to set stop-loss orders when opening a position and strictly adhere to them.

Many failed trades are not due to incorrect judgment, but rather a breakdown in discipline, such as temporarily canceling stop-loss orders when the market is falling, or constantly moving the stop-loss level down. Even if the price eventually recovers, in my opinion, this is still a failed trade because your trading system has been destroyed. Secondly, don't be misled by the concept of high leverage. Many people see nominal leverage of 100x, 150x, or 200x and think it's equivalent to going all in. But in reality, most professional traders use a full-margin approach. For example, with 100x leverage, opening only 1% of the position results in an actual leverage of only 1x. The purpose of high leverage is to improve capital utilization and facilitate simultaneous investment in multiple assets, not to amplify risk. Regarding the learning path, I've always believed that you don't need to learn too many technical analysis techniques. The book *Japanese Candlestick Charting* is sufficient to build a solid foundation. More importantly, it's crucial to fill in the gaps in macroeconomic understanding, such as basic macroeconomics and microeconomics, as well as sensitivity to important events like interest rate hikes, cuts, policy meetings, and international conflicts. These directly impact asset trends, such as the impact of war on gold prices. From A6 to A7, frankly, I had a lot of luck involved. I had just learned how to roll over positions and happened to catch the main upward wave of Bitcoin starting from $40,000. I added to my positions with profits, following the trend perfectly. I capitalized on this period very well. However, from A8 to A9, this was definitely the most difficult stage, and the most difficult stage of my entire trading career. This stage is very prone to mistakes. Many people get flustered and end up with their accounts wiped out, losing everything. And the disappointment from failure at this stage is enormous and extremely painful. This stage severely tests one's mindset and execution. The pressure is immense. Even if my overall position was in a profitable state before I went to sleep, the first thing I did every morning was check to see if my positions had been wiped out to break even. At that time, my partner and I were practically monitoring the market 24/7, even sleeping with headphones on. We'd call immediately at the first sign of a market signal, ready to execute it at any moment. It's hard to get through that without a strong heart.

Q: Looking ahead to the beginning of 2026, how do you view the cryptocurrency market this year? How will Bitcoin, Ethereum, and other mainstream coins perform? What trading opportunities or sectors do you particularly favor? Will you adjust your personal trading strategy this year?

Jeff Ma: I don't think this year will see a comprehensive breakout or extreme surge, but I also don't agree with the notion that it's just a one-sided bear market. Even in a bear market, it won't be a perpetual decline; there will definitely be periods of rebound. For me, it's not about just shorting, but about buying in batches at the right price levels to catch a sufficiently large rebound.

I mainly focus on Bitcoin, Ethereum, BNB, and SOL among the mainstream coins.

Buying Bitcoin in batches within the $60,000 to $84,000 range is perfectly acceptable; if a deep bear market truly arrives, I wouldn't be surprised if SOL falls below three digits, and it could fall very sharply. In the memecoin market, I will be very conservative. If I were to invest, I would likely only focus on Pepe and Doge, or similar tokens that have been repeatedly validated by the market. I generally avoid newly launched coins and projects whose logic I don't understand. Regarding overall asset allocation, a very important adjustment this year is increasing the allocation to gold and US stocks, and reducing the proportion of Web3. US stocks have been continuously hitting new highs recently, such as the S&P 500 index, and my ETF investments have already achieved significant returns. This cross-market allocation is crucial for reducing overall volatility and drawdowns.

data.title: Ripple Brings Blockchain Payments And Custody Directly Into Core Banking Systems Through DXC Partnership
data.description:
Ripple Moves From Outside Disruption To Inside Integration

Blockchain technology is no longer knocking on the doors of the world’s biggest banks; it has just been given a key to the building.

In a shift from the industry’s early disruptive phase, Ripple is now embedding its payment stack directly into the legacy systems that manage the global flow of money.

The strategy is coming to life through a landmark partnership with DXC Technology, a move that connects Ripple’s digital asset infrastructure, including the XRP Ledger and the RLUSD stablecoin, to the Hogan core banking platform.

For the uninitiated, Hogan is a titan of the traditional financial world, currently supporting more than 300 million deposit accounts and managing over $5 trillion in global deposits.

DXC Partners with @Ripple to Empower Global Banks with Scalable Digital Asset Custody and Payments

✅DXC integrates Ripple's institutional-grade blockchain technology into its Hogan core banking platform, which supports $5 trillion in deposits and 300 million accounts globally.… pic.twitter.com/JQ9LW50Odq

— Leonidas (@LeoHadjiloizou) January 21, 2026

By integrating at this level, Ripple is moving away from asking banks to experiment with separate, experimental sandboxes.

Instead, the technology is being woven into the operational heart of the banking sector, allowing institutions to process digital assets as part of their standard day-to-day routine.

Can Banks Adopt Blockchain Without Rebuilding Everything?

The most significant hurdle for institutional blockchain adoption has always been the rip and replace fear, the idea that using new technology requires tearing down decades of established infrastructure.

This integration changes that narrative.

Banks using Hogan can now access digital asset custody, tokenisation, and cross-border settlement as an extension of their existing workflows.

BIG NEWS: DXC partners with Ripple to integrate blockchain into Hogan core banking system ($5T deposits, 300M accounts) used by giants like Wells Fargo, Barclays, US Bank! Seamless custody & tokenization via RLUSD & XRP payments.

Massive bridge for TradFi & crypto! Moonshot for… pic.twitter.com/3Tv0XDEGL1

— Xaif Crypto🇮🇳|🇺🇸 (@Xaif_Crypto) January 21, 2026

Sandeep Bhanote, Global Head and General Manager of Financial Services at DXC, explains,

"Our work with Ripple brings those capabilities together in a way that allows banks to engage in the digital asset ecosystem without changing their core systems.”

He notes that the solution acts as a bridge, connecting traditional accounts and wallets to decentralised platforms at an enterprise scale.

This invisible upgrade means banks can offer programmable payments and manage stablecoins without the technical risk or internal friction typically associated with crypto-based projects.

It is a pragmatic approach where the blockchain handles the speed and cost of the transaction, while the bank’s core system remains the familiar interface for staff and regulators.

Why Is Ripple Investing In Trading Infrastructure And Research?

The DXC partnership is part of a much larger blueprint for institutional relevance.

On 15 January 2026, Ripple announced a $150 million financing agreement with LMAX Group, a major global cross-asset marketplace.

This deal positions Ripple’s stablecoin, RLUSD, as a core collateral asset for professional foreign exchange and digital asset trading.

🚨 JUST IN: #Ripple invests $150 million to support LMAX’s global growth strategy. pic.twitter.com/NwXraU3cFf

— RippleXity (@RippleXity) January 15, 2026

For LMAX, which saw $8.2 trillion in institutional volumes in 2025, the integration allows top-tier banks and brokers to use digital assets for more efficient margin funding and 24/7 cross-asset market access.

Simultaneously, Ripple is securing its future through academic foundations.

A recent $1.3 million commitment to the University of California, Berkeley, has launched the Center for Digital Assets.

Introducing the University Digital Asset Xcelerator (UDAX). 🎓@UCBerkeley and Ripple's University Blockchain Research Initiative launched a pilot program to accelerate the transition from academic innovation to institutional XRP utility: https://t.co/KMyVjPvXT2

The UDAX - UC…

— Ripple (@Ripple) January 16, 2026

This initiative, funded in RLUSD, focuses on applied research into digital twins and tokenisation, ensuring that the next generation of financial engineers is building on Ripple’s infrastructure.

Is This The End Of Experimental Blockchain?

For years, blockchain in banking felt like a perpetual pilot program.

This move suggests that the era of testing is over, replaced by production-ready deployment.

Joanie Xie, VP and Managing Director for North America at Ripple, highlights the pressure on traditional institutions to evolve.

Xie says,

"Banks are under increasing pressure to modernize while continuing to operate on complex infrastructure. Our partnership with DXC brings digital asset custody, RLUSD and payments directly into the core banking environments institutions already trust.”

She concluded,

"Together, we’re enabling banks to deliver secure, compliant digital asset use cases at enterprise scale without disruption.”

By targeting the software that runs the banks, Ripple is ensuring that digital assets like XRP and RLUSD aren't just speculative tools, but essential components of the global financial plumbing.

For the customer, the experience remains the same; for the bank, the settlement is faster and cheaper; and for the industry, the transition to digital finance has moved from a possibility to a quiet, integrated reality.
